examples.title: Algorithm: Inter-Cloud Resource Allocation
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating resource selection and allocation across multiple cloud providers based on cost and performance criteria
examples.code:
ALGORITHM InterCloudResourceAllocation
INPUT: workload_requirements, available_providers
OUTPUT: allocation_plan

BEGIN
    // Step 1: Initialize data structures
    SET allocation_plan = EMPTY
    SET selected_providers = EMPTY
    
    // Step 2: Discover available resources
    PRINT "Discovering available cloud resources..."
    FOR EACH provider IN available_providers:
        CALL QueryProviderAPI(provider) RETURNING resources
        ADD resources TO resource_catalog
    END FOR
    
    // Step 3: Filter providers meeting basic requirements
    PRINT "Filtering providers based on requirements..."
    SET qualified_providers = EMPTY
    FOR EACH resource IN resource_catalog:
        IF resource.meetsRequirements(workload_requirements) THEN
            ADD resource TO qualified_providers
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    // Step 4: Score each provider using multiple criteria
    PRINT "Scoring providers based on scheduling strategy..."
    FOR EACH provider IN qualified_providers:
        SET cost_score = CALL CalculateCostScore(provider, workload_requirements)
        SET performance_score = CALL CalculatePerformanceScore(provider)
        SET energy_score = CALL CalculateEnergyScore(provider)
        
        // Weighted scoring based on strategy
        SET strategy = workload_requirements.scheduling_strategy
        IF strategy = "cost_aware" THEN
            provider.total_score = (cost_score * 0.6) + (performance_score * 0.3) + (energy_score * 0.1)
        ELSE IF strategy = "performance_oriented" THEN
            provider.total_score = (performance_score * 0.6) + (cost_score * 0.3) + (energy_score * 0.1)
        ELSE IF strategy = "energy_efficient" THEN
            provider.total_score = (energy_score * 0.5) + (cost_score * 0.3) + (performance_score * 0.2)
        ELSE  // hybrid
            provider.total_score = (cost_score * 0.33) + (performance_score * 0.33) + (energy_score * 0.34)
        END IF
    END FOR
    
    // Step 5: Select optimal provider
    SET best_provider = provider with highest total_score
    
    // Step 6: Allocate resources
    PRINT "Allocating resources from selected provider..."
    CALL ProvisionResources(best_provider, workload_requirements)
    ADD (best_provider, workload_requirements) TO allocation_plan
    
    // Step 7: Update monitoring
    CALL UpdateResourceCatalog(best_provider)
    
    RETURN allocation_plan
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the resource allocation process in an inter-cloud environment. It shows resource discovery across multiple providers, filtering based on requirements, multi-criteria scoring (cost, performance, energy), and final resource provisioning. The algorithm supports different scheduling strategies: cost-aware, performance-oriented, energy-efficient, and hybrid.

examples.title: Python: Inter-Cloud Resource Manager
examples.description: Implementation of a multi-cloud resource management system with scheduling strategies and broker-based architecture
examples.code:
import time
from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import List, Dict, Optional

class SchedulingStrategy(Enum):
    COST_AWARE = "cost_aware"
    PERFORMANCE_ORIENTED = "performance_oriented"
    ENERGY_EFFICIENT = "energy_efficient"
    HYBRID = "hybrid"

@dataclass
class CloudProvider:
    name: str
    region: str
    cost_per_hour: float  # dollars
    performance_score: float  # 0-100
    energy_efficiency: float  # 0-100
    available_instances: int
    reliability: float  # 0-100

@dataclass
class Workload:
    name: str
    required_instances: int
    min_performance: float
    max_budget: float
    priority: str
    strategy: SchedulingStrategy

class InterCloudResourceManager:
    """Broker-based inter-cloud resource management system"""
    
    def __init__(self):
        self.providers: List[CloudProvider] = []
        self.resource_catalog: Dict[str, List] = {}
        self.allocations: List[Dict] = []
    
    def add_provider(self, provider: CloudProvider):
        """Register a new cloud provider"""
        self.providers.append(provider)
        self.resource_catalog[provider.name] = []
        print(f"[Broker] Registered provider: {provider.name} in {provider.region}")
    
    def discover_resources(self) -> Dict[str, List]:
        """Discover available resources across all providers""
        print("\n[Broker] Discovering resources across cloud providers...")
        
        for provider in self.providers:
            # Simulate resource discovery (in real implementation, would call provider APIs)
            available = provider.available_instances
            self.resource_catalog[provider.name] = [
                {"type": "compute", "available": available, "cost": provider.cost_per_hour}
                for _ in range(available)
            ]
            print(f"  - {provider.name}: {available} instances available @ ${provider.cost_per_hour}/hr")
        
        return self.resource_catalog
    
    def calculate_scores(self, provider: CloudProvider, workload: Workload) -> Dict[str, float]:
        """Calculate multi-criteria scores for a provider"""
        # Cost score (lower is better) - normalize to 0-100
        cost_score = max(0, 100 - (provider.cost_per_hour * 10))
        
        # Performance score (higher is better)
        performance_score = provider.performance_score
        
        # Energy score (higher is better)
        energy_score = provider.energy_efficiency
        
        return {
            "cost": cost_score,
            "performance": performance_score,
            "energy": energy_score
        }
    
    def select_provider(self, workload: Workload) -> Optional[CloudProvider]:
        """Select optimal provider based on scheduling strategy"""
        qualified = [p for p in self.providers 
                     if p.available_instances >= workload.required_instances
                     and p.performance_score >= workload.min_performance]
        
        if not qualified:
            print(f"[Broker] No provider meets requirements for workload: {workload.name}")
            return None
        
        best_provider = None
        best_score = -1
        
        for provider in qualified:
            scores = self.calculate_scores(provider, workload)
            
            # Apply scoring weights based on strategy
            if workload.strategy == SchedulingStrategy.COST_AWARE:
                total = scores["cost"] * 0.6 + scores["performance"] * 0.3 + scores["energy"] * 0.1
            elif workload.strategy == SchedulingStrategy.PERFORMANCE_ORIENTED:
                total = scores["performance"] * 0.6 + scores["cost"] * 0.3 + scores["energy"] * 0.1
            elif workload.strategy == SchedulingStrategy.ENERGY_EFFICIENT:
                total = scores["energy"] * 0.5 + scores["cost"] * 0.3 + scores["performance"] * 0.2
            else:  # HYBRID
                total = (scores["cost"] + scores["performance"] + scores["energy"]) / 3
            
            if total > best_score:
                best_score = total
                best_provider = provider
        
        return best_provider
    
    def allocate_resources(self, workload: Workload) -> bool:
        """Allocate resources to workload from optimal provider""
        print(f"\n[Broker] Processing allocation for workload: {workload.name}")
        
        # Discover resources first
        self.discover_resources()
        
        # Select best provider
        provider = self.select_provider(workload)
        
        if provider is None:
            return False
        
        # Check budget constraint
        estimated_cost = provider.cost_per_hour * workload.required_instances
        if estimated_cost > workload.max_budget:
            print(f"[Broker] Budget exceeded: ${estimated_cost:.2f} > ${workload.max_budget:.2f}")
            return False
        
        # Allocate resources
        provider.available_instances -= workload.required_instances
        
        allocation = {
            "workload": workload.name,
            "provider": provider.name,
            "instances": workload.required_instances,
            "cost_per_hour": estimated_cost,
            "timestamp": time.time()
        }
        self.allocations.append(allocation)
        
        print(f"[Broker] Successfully allocated {workload.required_instances} instances")
        print(f"  Provider: {provider.name}")
        print(f"  Strategy: {workload.strategy.value}")
        print(f"  Cost: ${estimated_cost:.2f}/hour")
        
        return True
    
    def monitor_allocations(self):
        """Monitor current resource allocations"""
        print("\n[Broker] Current Resource Allocations:")
        for alloc in self.allocations:
            print(f"  - {alloc['workload']}: {alloc['instances']} instances @ {alloc['provider']}")

# Demo: Cloud Bursting Scenario
def cloud_bursting_demo():
    print("=" * 60)
    print("CLOUD BURSTING DEMO - E-Commerce Peak Demand")
    print("=" * 60)
    
    # Initialize broker
    broker = InterCloudResourceManager()
    
    # Register cloud providers
    broker.add_provider(CloudProvider(
        name="PrivateCloud",
        region="us-east",
        cost_per_hour=5.0,
        performance_score=85,
        energy_efficiency=90,
        available_instances=50,
        reliability=98
    ))
    
    broker.add_provider(CloudProvider(
        name="AWS-Public",
        region="us-east-1",
        cost_per_hour=12.0,
        performance_score=95,
        energy_efficiency=70,
        available_instances=1000,
        reliability=99
    ))
    
    broker.add_provider(CloudProvider(
        name="Azure-Public",
        region="eastus",
        cost_per_hour=10.0,
        performance_score=90,
        energy_efficiency=75,
        available_instances=800,
        reliability=99
    ))
    
    # Normal operation workload
    normal_workload = Workload(
        name="Ecommerce-App",
        required_instances=30,
        min_performance=70,
        max_budget=500,
        priority="high",
        strategy=SchedulingStrategy.COST_AWARE
    )
    
    print("\n--- Normal Operation ---")
    broker.allocate_resources(normal_workload)
    
    # Peak demand workload (bursting)
    peak_workload = Workload(
        name="Holiday-Sale-App",
        required_instances=200,
        min_performance=80,
        max_budget=3000,
        priority="critical",
        strategy=SchedulingStrategy.PERFORMANCE_ORIENTED
    )
    
    print("\n--- Peak Demand (Cloud Bursting) ---")
    broker.allocate_resources(peak_workload)
    
    # Show final allocations
    broker.monitor_allocations()

if __name__ == "__main__":
    cloud_bursting_demo()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation demonstrates a broker-based inter-cloud resource management system. It includes CloudProvider and Workload data classes, resource discovery across multiple providers, multi-criteria scoring based on different scheduling strategies (cost-aware, performance-oriented, energy-efficient, hybrid), and resource allocation with budget checking. The demo shows a cloud bursting scenario for e-commerce peak demand handling.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n*m) where n is providers and m is workload requirements
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n) for provider storage and allocations

examples.title: C: Inter-Cloud Broker System
examples.description: Implementation of a broker-based inter-cloud resource allocation system in C with provider management and scheduling
examples.explanation: This C implementation demonstrates a broker-based inter-cloud resource management system. It includes structures for cloud providers, workloads, and allocations. The system supports provider registration, multi-criteria scoring based on different scheduling strategies, resource allocation with budget checking, and allocation tracking. The main function demonstrates cloud bursting with multiple workloads using different strategies.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for provider selection where n is number of providers
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n + m) for provider and allocation storage
